name: Guinea-Bissau
tagline: From the Mandinka empire of Kaabu and the coastal peoples of the Rivers of Guinea, through Portuguese slaving forts, a very late colonial conquest, and Amílcar Cabral's guerrilla war, to independence in 1973–1974 and decades of coups.
summary: Guinea-Bissau is a small West African nation of mainland river country and the offshore Bijagós archipelago, wedged between Senegal and Guinea. Before the Europeans came, its interior lay within the reach of the Mali Empire and then of Kaabu (Gabu), a Mandinka federation of warrior nobility that long outlasted Mali, while the coast and islands were home to the Balanta, Fula, Papel, Manjaco, Biafada and the seafaring Bijagós. From the mid-fifteenth century Portuguese navigators reached the "Rivers of Guinea", and the region — administered for centuries through Cape Verde — became an artery of the Atlantic slave trade centred on Cacheu and, later, Bissau. Portugal's actual conquest of the interior came strikingly late, completed only through brutal "pacification" campaigns into the 1910s and the subjection of the Bijagós islands by 1936. Amílcar Cabral's PAIGC waged one of Africa's most effective anti-colonial guerrilla wars from 1963; Cabral was assassinated in January 1973, the party declared independence on 24 September 1973, and Portugal recognised it on 10 September 1974 after the Carnation Revolution. Independence gave way to chronic instability — a 1980 coup, the 1998–1999 civil war, repeated coups, and a notorious role as a transit point for cocaine bound from Latin America to Europe.

founding.label: Independence from Portugal
founding.detail: On 24 September 1973 the PAIGC's People's National Assembly, meeting in the liberated southeastern region of Boé, unilaterally proclaimed the Republic of Guinea-Bissau, some eight months after the assassination of the party's founder, Amílcar Cabral, in January 1973. The new state was swiftly recognised by dozens of countries and endorsed by a 93-to-7 vote of the UN General Assembly in November 1973, though Portugal still claimed sovereignty. That changed after the Carnation Revolution of 25 April 1974 toppled the Estado Novo dictatorship in Lisbon: the guerrilla war in Guinea had been among the conflicts that exhausted the regime. The new Portuguese government negotiated with the PAIGC, signing an accord at Algiers in August 1974, and on 10 September 1974 Portugal formally recognised Guinea-Bissau's independence. The United States acknowledged independence the same day. Guinea-Bissau thus became independent twice over — by its own declaration in 1973, and by Portuguese recognition in 1974 — making it the first of Portugal's African colonies to win statehood.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Declared 24 September 1973; recognised by Portugal on 10 September 1974, after the Carnation Revolution

quickFacts.label: Liberation movement
quickFacts.value: PAIGC (African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde), founded 1956 by Amílcar Cabral

quickFacts.label: First president
quickFacts.value: Luís Cabral (1974–1980), half-brother of Amílcar Cabral, overthrown in a 1980 coup

quickFacts.label: Pre-colonial state
quickFacts.value: Kaabu (Gabu), a Mandinka federation and successor of the Mali Empire, destroyed at the Battle of Kansala in 1867

quickFacts.label: Colonial era
quickFacts.value: "Portuguese Guinea" — administered via Cape Verde until 1879; interior conquered only c. 1912–1936

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Bissau, founded in 1765 as a fortified slave-trading post; replaced Bolama as capital in 1942

eras.title: Kaabu and the peoples of the Rivers of Guinea
eras.standfirst: The interior fell within the Mali Empire and then to Kaabu, a Mandinka federation of warrior nobility, while the coast and islands held the Balanta, Fula, Papel, Manjaco, Biafada and the seafaring Bijagós.
body: In the thirteenth century the savanna of what is now eastern Guinea-Bissau was drawn into the orbit of the Mali Empire, the great Mande state of the upper Niger. Tradition holds that one of Sundiata Keita's generals, Tiramakhan (Tiramang) Traoré, led the western expansion, and the conquered lands became Kaabu (also spelled Gabu), a province of Mali. As Mali declined from the fourteenth century, Kaabu became an independent federation of Mandinka kingdoms — the most powerful western Mande state of its era — extending across northeastern Guinea-Bissau and into the Casamance and the Gambia. It was ruled by a warrior aristocracy, the Nyancho (Ñaanco), who claimed descent from Tiramakhan and excelled as cavalry warriors, with a capital at Kansala in the Gabú region.

The Atlantic coast and the lower river valleys were home to a mosaic of other peoples whose social orders differed sharply from the Mandinka hierarchy. The Balanta and the Jola lived largely without kings, organising life around the village and the lineage; the Fula (Fulani), Papel (Pepel), Manjaco (Manjak), Biafada and others lived under more centralised chieftaincies. Offshore, the Bijagós of the archipelago that bears their name developed distinctive matrilineal warrior societies and were renowned seafarers and raiders, long resisting outside control. This was the "Guinea of the Rivers" — a labyrinth of tidal creeks, estuaries and islands where political power was fragmented among many small states and peoples.

Kaabu's wealth was tied to trade, including the traffic in enslaved people, and its elite acquired firearms and imported goods through the coastal commerce. In the nineteenth century the federation came under mounting pressure from the Muslim Fula state of Futa Jallon to the southeast and from Fula populations within its own borders. In 1867 a Fula army associated with Alfa Molo besieged the Kaabu capital of Kansala; according to tradition the Mandinka ruler (mansaba) Dianke Walli, facing defeat after a long siege, set fire to the town's gunpowder magazine, destroying the city and its defenders. The fall of Kansala ended Kaabu's independence and cleared the way for the Fula kingdom of Fuladu.

events.title: Mali's reach and the rise of Kaabu
events.summary: The Mali Empire expanded west into the region, traditionally under Sundiata Keita's general Tiramakhan Traoré; the conquered lands became Kaabu (Gabu), a Mandinka province that would outlast Mali itself.

events.title: Kaabu becomes an independent federation
events.summary: As Mali declined, Kaabu emerged as an independent federation of Mandinka kingdoms led by the Nyancho warrior nobility, centred on Kansala and dominant across the Senegambian interior.

events.title: The coastal and island peoples
events.summary: The coast and rivers held the Balanta, Jola, Fula, Papel, Manjaco and Biafada, while the offshore Bijagós formed matrilineal warrior societies of seafarers and raiders — a fragmented mosaic of peoples and small states.

events.title: The Battle of Kansala ends Kaabu
events.summary: A Fula army linked to Alfa Molo besieged and destroyed the Kaabu capital of Kansala; tradition holds the mansaba Dianke Walli blew up the gunpowder store rather than surrender, ending Kaabu and giving rise to the Fula kingdom of Fuladu.

pullquote.text: Born as a province of Mali, Kaabu outlived the empire that made it — a federation of warrior nobility that endured until the magazines of Kansala were set ablaze in 1867.

eras.title: The Rivers of Guinea and the Atlantic slave trade
eras.standfirst: From the mid-fifteenth century Portuguese ships reached the Rivers of Guinea; administered through Cape Verde, the coast became an artery of the Atlantic slave trade centred on Cacheu and Bissau.
body: The estuaries of Guinea were among the first parts of West Africa reached by Portuguese navigators. In 1455–1456 the Venetian Alvise Cadamosto, sailing for Prince Henry the Navigator, explored the coast and rivers, followed by Diogo Gomes and other captains. The Portuguese did not at first settle the mainland in force; instead the early trade was carried on by lançados and tangomãos — often exiles, New Christians and their Afro-Portuguese descendants — who married into local communities and brokered commerce in the creeks. The whole region, the "Rios de Guiné" or Rivers of Guinea, was for centuries governed not on its own terms but as a dependency of the Cape Verde islands offshore.

From the sixteenth century the trade turned increasingly to human beings. Captives drawn from the interior and the coastal wars were carried first to Cape Verde — where tens of thousands were taken to develop the islands' plantation economy — and onward across the Atlantic, with many shipped to the Spanish Americas, especially during the Iberian Union of the Portuguese and Spanish crowns (1580–1640). Cacheu, fortified around 1588, became the principal slaving station on the rivers; other ports such as Guinala and, later, Bissau fed the same traffic. The Portuguese established a captaincy to administer the trade, but their authority rarely extended beyond the forts and trading towns.

Bissau itself was founded in 1765 as a fortified military and slave-trading post, growing into the region's main commercial centre. The transatlantic slave trade declined across the nineteenth century as the European powers moved to abolish it; Portugal ended legal slaving and slavery in stages, abolishing slavery in its territories in 1869 and replacing it with coercive "contract labour" systems. Even so, for four centuries Portuguese power in Guinea had rested on commerce, alliance and the forts of the coast, not on rule of the land — a colony that was, in practice, a string of trading posts among independent African states.

events.title: Portuguese ships reach the Rivers of Guinea
events.summary: Expeditions under Alvise Cadamosto, sailing for Portugal's Prince Henry the Navigator, explored the coast and estuaries of Guinea, opening European trade in the labyrinth of rivers that gave the region its name.

events.title: Cacheu fortified as a slaving station
events.summary: The Portuguese established a fortified post at Cacheu, which became the principal slave-trading centre on the rivers; captives were funnelled through it to Cape Verde and across the Atlantic.

events.title: The Atlantic slave trade via Cape Verde
events.summary: Administered through Cape Verde, the Rivers of Guinea became a major source of enslaved people; tens of thousands were carried to the islands' plantations and onward to the Americas, especially during the Iberian Union (1580–1640).

events.title: Bissau founded as a fortified post
events.summary: The Portuguese founded Bissau in 1765 as a military and slave-trading station; it grew into the region's main commercial centre and, in 1942, replaced Bolama as the colonial capital.

events.title: Slavery abolished, contract labour follows
events.summary: Portugal abolished slavery in its territories, but replaced it with coercive "contract labour" arrangements; the transatlantic trade through Guinea's rivers had already declined across the nineteenth century.

pullquote.text: For four centuries Portuguese Guinea was a string of forts and trading towns among independent African states — a coast administered from the Cape Verde islands offshore.

eras.title: Portuguese Guinea and the late conquest
eras.standfirst: Separated from Cape Verde in 1879, the colony was "pacified" only very late: a brutal conquest of the interior into the 1910s and the subjection of the Bijagós islands by 1936.
body: In 1879 Portuguese Guinea was administratively separated from Cape Verde and constituted as a distinct colony, and after the Berlin Conference of 1884–1885 its boundaries with French territory were fixed by negotiation with France. Yet for decades these borders existed mainly on European maps. African rulers and communities still held the countryside; the Portuguese governed little more than the coastal forts, river ports and trading posts of Bissau, Cacheu, Bolama and Geba. Lisbon made Guinea a separate military district in 1892 in an effort to enforce "effective occupation", but the colony remained, for a long time, a nominal possession.

The real conquest came strikingly late and was correspondingly violent. To meet the European standard of "effective occupation" — and to extract taxes and labour — Portugal launched a series of "pacification" campaigns in the early twentieth century. From 1912 the army officer João Teixeira Pinto, aided by the Senegalese auxiliary commander Abdul Injai, crushed mainland resistance with great brutality across the mid-1910s. The seafaring Bijagós of the archipelago held out longest of all, subdued only through repeated campaigns; the Estado Novo finally imposed control over the islands in 1935–1936, more than four centuries after the first Portuguese ships had appeared off the coast.

Even after conquest, Portuguese Guinea remained a poor and neglected colony whose administrative costs outran its revenues. The Salazar regime entrenched a forced-labour and forced-crop system and, in 1951, in response to mounting international criticism of colonialism, renamed its colonies "overseas provinces" (províncias ultramarinas) in an attempt to recast empire as an indivisible part of Portugal. For the colonised, little changed: Guinea remained one of the most underdeveloped territories in Africa, with almost no schools, scant infrastructure and a tiny assimilated elite — the conditions in which an organised nationalist movement would soon take root.

events.title: Guinea separated from Cape Verde
events.summary: Portuguese Guinea was detached from Cape Verde and constituted as a separate colony; after the Berlin Conference its frontiers with French possessions were fixed by agreement with France.

events.title: A separate military district
events.summary: Lisbon made Guinea a distinct military district to promote "effective occupation", but Portuguese authority still reached little beyond the coastal forts and trading towns.

events.title: Teixeira Pinto's pacification of the mainland
events.summary: The officer João Teixeira Pinto, with the auxiliary Abdul Injai, led brutal campaigns that crushed mainland resistance and brought the interior under Portuguese control for the first time.

events.title: The Bijagós islands subdued
events.summary: After repeated campaigns against the seafaring Bijagós, the Estado Novo finally imposed control over the archipelago in 1935–1936, completing the conquest more than four centuries after first Portuguese contact.

events.title: Renamed an "overseas province"
events.summary: Responding to international criticism, Portugal reclassified Guinea and its other colonies as "overseas provinces", recasting empire as an integral part of Portugal while colonial conditions persisted.

pullquote.text: More than four centuries after the first Portuguese ships, the conquest of the interior was finished only in the 1910s, and the Bijagós islands not until 1936.

eras.title: Cabral, the PAIGC and the war of independence
eras.standfirst: Amílcar Cabral's PAIGC waged one of Africa's most effective guerrilla wars from 1963; Cabral was assassinated in January 1973, independence was declared that September, and Portugal recognised it in 1974.
body: In 1956 the agronomist Amílcar Cabral, born in Bafatá in 1924 and trained in Lisbon, founded the African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde (PAIGC) together with a small group of nationalists. The party at first sought peaceful change, but the Pidjiguiti massacre of 3 August 1959 — when Portuguese police fired on striking dockworkers at the Pidjiguiti quay in Bissau, killing scores — convinced Cabral and the PAIGC that only armed struggle could end colonial rule. The party turned to the countryside, building support among the peasantry and training a guerrilla army with bases in neighbouring Guinea (Conakry).

The war began in January 1963 with PAIGC attacks on Portuguese garrisons in the south. It proved one of the most effective anti-colonial campaigns in Africa: combining patient political organisation with guerrilla tactics, the PAIGC steadily expanded the "liberated zones", running schools, clinics and people's stores in areas it controlled. By the late 1960s it dominated much of the rural interior, and with Soviet, Chinese and Cuban support — including, after 1968, shoulder-fired SA-7 anti-aircraft missiles — it broke Portugal's air superiority. Cabral became an internationally admired theorist of national liberation, even as the Portuguese army, under General António de Spínola, fought to hold the colony.

On 20 January 1973, after leaving a reception in Conakry, Cabral was shot dead at a PAIGC roadblock by a disgruntled party member, Inocêncio Kani, and others, in a plot widely linked to Portuguese intelligence. The movement survived the blow. On 24 September 1973 the PAIGC's People's National Assembly proclaimed the independent Republic of Guinea-Bissau in the liberated region of Boé, and dozens of states quickly recognised it. The decisive change came in Lisbon: the Carnation Revolution of 25 April 1974, driven in part by exhaustion with the African wars, overthrew the Estado Novo. After an accord signed at Algiers, Portugal recognised Guinea-Bissau's independence on 10 September 1974, with Amílcar's half-brother Luís Cabral as the new republic's first president.

events.title: Amílcar Cabral founds the PAIGC
events.summary: The agronomist Amílcar Cabral and a small group founded the African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde (PAIGC), the movement that would lead the struggle against Portuguese rule.

events.title: The Pidjiguiti massacre
events.summary: Portuguese police opened fire on striking dockworkers at the Pidjiguiti quay in Bissau, killing scores; the killings turned the PAIGC away from peaceful protest and toward armed struggle.

events.title: The war of independence begins
events.summary: The PAIGC launched its armed campaign with attacks on Portuguese garrisons in the south, opening a guerrilla war that would steadily expand the "liberated zones" across the interior.

events.title: Cabral assassinated in Conakry
events.summary: Amílcar Cabral was shot dead at a roadblock in Conakry by a disgruntled PAIGC member, Inocêncio Kani, and others, in a plot widely linked to Portuguese agents — eight months before independence was proclaimed.

events.title: Independence declared at Boé
events.summary: The PAIGC's People's National Assembly unilaterally proclaimed the Republic of Guinea-Bissau in the liberated southeast; the new state was rapidly recognised abroad and endorsed by a 93–7 UN General Assembly vote in November.

events.title: Portugal recognises independence
events.summary: Following the Carnation Revolution and an accord at Algiers, Portugal formally recognised Guinea-Bissau's independence on 10 September 1974; the United States acknowledged it the same day, with Luís Cabral as first president.

figures.name: Amílcar Cabral
figures.role: Founder and leader of the PAIGC
figures.blurb: Agronomist, poet and revolutionary theorist who founded the PAIGC in 1956 and led one of Africa's most effective anti-colonial guerrilla wars from his base in Conakry. Internationally admired as a thinker on national liberation and culture, he was assassinated at a roadblock in Conakry on 20 January 1973, eight months before independence was proclaimed.

pullquote.text: By the late 1960s the PAIGC ran schools, clinics and stores across liberated zones it controlled — a state already taking shape inside the colony.

eras.title: Instability, civil war and the cocaine route
eras.standfirst: Independence gave way to chronic instability — a 1980 coup, the 1998–1999 civil war, repeated coups and assassinations, and a notorious role as a transit point for cocaine bound from Latin America to Europe.
body: Luís Cabral's one-party PAIGC government lasted barely six years. In November 1980 a coup led by the prime minister and armed-forces commander João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira overthrew him, fracturing the union that had bound Guinea-Bissau and Cape Verde and putting Vieira at the centre of national politics for nearly three decades. Multi-party politics were introduced in 1991, and the first competitive elections in 1994 confirmed Vieira as president, but the underlying fragility of the state — a politicised, faction-ridden military and a weak economy — remained.

In June 1998 a mutiny by a dismissed army chief, Ansumane Mané, escalated into the Guinea-Bissau Civil War. Fighting that drew in Senegalese and Guinean troops on Vieira's side devastated Bissau and displaced hundreds of thousands of people before Vieira was ousted by the military in May 1999. The years that followed were a cycle of coups, mutinies and assassinations: President Kumba Ialá was overthrown in 2003; the armed-forces chief Veríssimo Correia Seabra was killed in a 2004 mutiny; Vieira returned to the presidency in 2005 only to be assassinated by soldiers in March 2009, hours after the killing of the army chief of staff; and a further coup in April 2012 interrupted a presidential election.

From the mid-2000s Guinea-Bissau acquired a grim new reputation. Its weak institutions, porous borders, archipelago of uninhabited islands and chronically unpaid security forces made it an ideal transit point for Latin American cocaine bound for Europe, and the UN and others described it as the first African "narco-state", with the value of the drug trade rivalling the entire legal economy. Trafficking interests became entangled with elements of the military and political class, deepening instability; failed coup attempts in 2022 and 2023 were officially linked to the drug trade, and in late 2025 the military again seized power. Guinea-Bissau remains one of the world's poorest countries, its democratic institutions fragile and its history since independence shadowed by the gun and the smuggling vessel.

events.title: Nino Vieira seizes power
events.summary: A coup led by João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira overthrew President Luís Cabral, fracturing the planned union with Cape Verde and beginning Vieira's dominance of national politics.

events.title: First multi-party elections
events.summary: After the introduction of multi-party politics in 1991, Guinea-Bissau held its first competitive elections in 1994, which confirmed Vieira as president — though military factionalism persisted.

events.title: The civil war
events.summary: A military mutiny led by Ansumane Mané escalated into a civil war that drew in Senegalese and Guinean troops, devastated Bissau and displaced hundreds of thousands before Vieira was ousted in May 1999.

events.title: Vieira assassinated
events.summary: Having returned to the presidency in 2005, João Bernardo Vieira was assassinated by soldiers in March 2009, hours after the killing of the army chief of staff — a vivid sign of the army's grip on politics.

events.title: Coup amid the cocaine trade
events.summary: A military coup interrupted a presidential election in April 2012, deepening a pattern of intervention by a politicised army increasingly entangled with cocaine trafficking.

events.title: A cocaine transit "narco-state"
events.summary: Weak institutions, porous borders and an archipelago of uninhabited islands made Guinea-Bissau a major transit point for Latin American cocaine bound for Europe; the UN described it as the first African "narco-state", with the drug trade rivalling the legal economy.

figures.name: Luís Cabral
figures.blurb: Half-brother of Amílcar Cabral and a PAIGC leader who became the independent republic's first president in 1974, governing a one-party state until he was overthrown in the 1980 coup led by Nino Vieira.

figures.name: João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira
figures.blurb: A PAIGC war veteran who seized power in 1980 and dominated politics for decades; ousted in the 1998–1999 civil war, he returned as elected president in 2005 before being assassinated by soldiers in March 2009.

pullquote.text: Weak institutions, porous borders and uninhabited islands made the country a transit point for cocaine — the UN called it the first African "narco-state".

figures.name: Amílcar Cabral
figures.role: Founder of the PAIGC and leader of the independence struggle
figures.blurb: Agronomist and revolutionary theorist who founded the PAIGC in 1956 and led one of Africa's most effective anti-colonial guerrilla wars until his assassination in Conakry in January 1973, eight months before Guinea-Bissau proclaimed independence.

figures.name: Luís Cabral
figures.blurb: Amílcar Cabral's half-brother and PAIGC leader who became the independent republic's first president in 1974 and was overthrown in the 1980 coup.

figures.name: João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira
figures.blurb: War veteran who seized power in 1980, dominated politics for decades, was ousted in the 1998–1999 civil war, returned as elected president in 2005, and was assassinated by soldiers in 2009.

sources.label: A Guide to the History of Recognition: Guinea-Bissau
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.publisher: UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)

sources.label: Guinea-Bissau's illegal drug economy
sources.publisher: Global Initiative Against Transnational Organized Crime
